ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 13, 7, 952664, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL

- `project` text NULL

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- `go_live` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL

- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 13, 7, 952664, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

Many target fields like ERP_Number__c, Website, BillingCity, BillingCountry, CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted don't exist in source. I'll need to provide NULLs or defaults for these.

For the Account model:
- Id = 'ACC-' + numeric part (from id column) → Actually wait, let me re-check. The source id is already "ACC-1001". But the target expects Salesforce-style Id. Should I keep it as-is or transform it?

Actually, looking at the guidelines more carefully:
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key

So I think the approach is:
- Transform account.id (ACC-1001) to a Salesforce-style ID format
- Or maybe just use it as-is since that IS the account identifier

Wait, let me reconsider. The rules say "not raw source customer numbers" for AccountId/Account__c references in related tables. For the Account model itself, the Id column should be... the account's own id? Let me think about this.

For Contact.AccountId: This should reference the Account.Id. If I map ACC-1001 → something, it should be consistent everywhere.
For Opportunity.AccountId: Similarly reference Account.Id
For Project.Account__c and Installed_Asset__c.Account__c: Same

The "not raw source customer numbers" means don't use KD-XXXX (the customer_number from opportunity) as the AccountId - use the proper account ID mapping instead.

I think the simplest approach is to keep the Account Id as the transformed id value. Let me check what format makes sense - maybe I should transform ACC-1001 into a Salesforce-style format or just keep it.

Actually, looking at it more practically: since this is a dbt transformation from source data, and the source account id is "ACC-1001", the target Account.Id could simply be derived from that. The key thing is consistency across all models.

Let me decide on a pattern:
- Source account.id = "ACC-1001" → Target Account.Id = same or transformed? 
- For KD mapping: opportunity.customer_number = "KD-1001" → extract number 1001, join to account where id starts with 'ACC-' and numeric part matches

I think I'll keep Account.Id as the source id value (or maybe prefix/suffix it to make it Salesforce-like). Let me check if there's any standard pattern...

Actually, let me just use a straightforward approach:
- Account.Id = source account.id as-is (e.g., "ACC-1001")  
- For references from other tables: 
  - Contact.AccountId = account_ref transformed to match Account.Id format
  - Opportunity.AccountId = derived by extracting number from customer_number and joining to account
  - Project.Account__c = client_id → same format as Account.Id
  - Asset.Account__c = client if ACC-XXXX, or matched by name

Wait, but the guidelines say "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id, not raw source customer numbers." This means I need to:
1. Define what the Salesforce-style Account.Id looks like
2. Make sure all references point to that consistently

I think the safest approach is:
- Transform account id "ACC-1001" to something consistent - maybe just use it as-is since it already looks standardized
- For opportunity.customer_number "KD-1001": extract the number and map to account by matching numeric suffix → Account.Id = 'ACC-' + number

Actually, you know what - let me simplify. The source id values in accounts are already prefixed with "ACC-" and look like proper identifiers. Let me just use them directly. For other tables:
- Contact.account_ref is already "ACC-XXXX" format → direct join to Account.Id
- Project.client_id is already "ACC-XXXX" format → direct join to Account.Id  
- Opportunity.customer_number is "KD-XXXX" → extract XXXX, match to Account.id where RIGHT(id, length) matches

For asset client:
- If starts with 'ACC-': direct reference to Account.Id
- Otherwise: try to match by name (LEFT JOIN and find matching account.name)

Let me now also think about what I'm missing:
- Many target fields don't exist in source → will use NULL or sensible defaults
- ERP_Number__c, Website, BillingCity, BillingCountry for Account → NULL
- CreatedDate, LastModifiedDate, IsDeleted → probably not in source data
- For Contact: Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c → likely NULL or default

Let me verify what fields are actually available by checking the exact source columns again and then proceed to write the models.

Actually, I already have the column lists from the prompt. Let me now focus on writing the models. For fields not in source:
- Use NULL as default
- For NOT NULL constraints, use sensible defaults ('Unknown', etc.)

Let me also check if there's any data about ERP numbers, websites, billing info, phone numbers, etc. anywhere.

OK let me just check a few more things quickly and then write all 5 models.', id='reasoning_content', provider_name='openai'), ToolCallPart(tool_name='query_postgres', args='{"sql": "SELECT DISTINCT account_ref FROM \"fixture_missing_relations_v2_src\".\"contact\" WHERE account_ref IS NOT NULL ORDER BY account_ref LIMIT 20"}', tool_call_id='call_z2i39cwj')], usage=RequestUsage(input_tokens=11596, output_tokens=1208), model_name='ise-ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 16, 46, 801401, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), provider_name='openai', provider_url='http://localhost:4000/v1/', provider_details={'finish_reason': 'tool_calls', 'timestamp': datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 16, 46, tzinfo=TzInfo(0))}, provider_response_id='chatcmpl-ae4eb10f-560a-4906-8974-c064e2f9d4fe', finish_reason='tool_call')

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 13, 7, 952664, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

Key decisions:

2. **ERP_Number__c**, **Website**, **BillingCity**, **BillingCountry**, **CreatedDate**, **LastModifiedDate**, **IsDeleted**: Not in source → NULL for nullable, 'Unknown'/'0' for NOT NULL where needed.

4. **Contact.Id**: Source id "CON-XXXX"
5. **Contact.FirstName/LastName**: Split full_name by first space
6. **Contact.Phone, Title, Role__c, Preferred_Language__c**: Not in source → NULL/default
7. **Contact.AccountId**: Map account_ref directly (already ACC-XXXX format)
8. **Opportunity.Id**: Source id "OPP-XXXX"
9. **Opportunity.StageName**: Map from stage enum - need to handle mapping for any non-standard values
10. **Opportunity.CloseDate**: Need to check if this date exists in source... Looking at opportunity columns: id, name, stage, amount, customer_number, account_name. No CloseDate column! → NULL or default
11. **Opportunity.CurrencyIsoCode**: Not in source → NULL or 'EUR'
12. **Opportunity.AccountId**: Extract number from customer_number "KD-XXXX" and join to Account.Id 'ACC-' + that number
13. **Project__c.Id**: Source id "PROJ-XXXX"
14. **Project__c.Project_Status__c**: Map from status (Active/Completed/In Planning/On Hold/Cancelled already match)
15. **Project__c.Go_Live_Date__c**: From go_live, already ISO format
16. **Project__c.Account__c**: Join client_id to Account.Id (already ACC-XXXX format)
17. **Project__c.Opportunity__c**: Join opportunity_ref to Opportunity.Id (already OPP-XXXX format)
18. **Installed_Asset__c.Id**: Source id "AST-XXXX"
19. **Installed_Asset__c.Account__c**: For ACC-XXXX format, direct join; for company names, try name match or NULL
20. **Installed_Asset__c.Project__c**: Join project to Project.id (already PROJ-XXXX format)

For enum mappings:
- Customer_Tier__c: Gold→Gold, Silver→Silver, Bronze→Bronze, Platinum→Platinum (already match)
- Contact Role__c: Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor → No source data → NULL or default like 'End User'

- Project Status: Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled → All match source values

Let me now write all the model files. Let me also think about what's NOT NULL in target:

Account: Id (NOT NULL), Name (NOT NULL)
Contact: Id (NOT NULL), LastName (NOT NULL) 
Opportunity: Id (NOT NULL), Name (NOT NULL), StageName (NOT NULL), CloseDate (NOT NULL)

Installed_Asset__c: Id (NOT NULL), Name (NOT NULL)

For NOT NULL fields where source might be empty, I'll use sensible defaults.

Now let me write the models. I need to create:
1. models/account.sql
2. models/contact.sql
3. models/opportunity.sql
4. models/project__c.sql (note the double underscore in target name)
5. models/installed_asset__c.sql
6. models/sources.yml

Wait, I also need to handle the Account Id format for references from other tables. Let me make sure:
- Source account id = "ACC-1001" → This is Account.Id in target
- Contact.account_ref = "ACC-1001" → Maps directly to Account.Id ✓
- Project.client_id = "ACC-1001" → Maps directly to Account.Id ✓
- Opportunity.customer_number = "KD-1001" → Need to extract 1001, then Account.Id = 'ACC-' || '1001'

For opportunity AccountId mapping:
```sql
LEFT JOIN "{{ source_schema }}"."account" acct ON RIGHT(opps.customer_number, LENGTH(opps.customer_number) - 3) = SUBSTRING(acct.id FROM '\d+')
```
Or more simply: `SUBSTRING(opps.customer_number FROM '\d+') = SUBSTRING(acct.id FROM '\d+')`

For asset Account__c:
- If client starts with 'ACC-': join to account where id = client
- Else: try LEFT JOIN on name (account.name = asset.client) - this is fuzzy but that's the data we have

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing a dbt project on disk.

You have these tools:
- write_file(path, content) — write a file under the dbt project dir
- read_file(path) — read a file you've written
- (optionally) query_postgres(sql) — run a read-only SELECT against staging

Layout you must produce:
- models/<target_table>.sql — one per target table (paths are relative to the dbt project root, not dbt_project/models/)
- models/sources.yml — declare every source used by your models

Raw source data lives in the physical Postgres source schema shown in context. Transformed dbt models materialize into a per-run output schema at run time.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.

Stop when the dbt project is complete.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 11, 13, 13, 7, 952664, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_missing_relations_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_missing_relations_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.account  (80 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `tier` text NULL
- `region` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.asset  (320 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial` text NULL
- `warranty` text NULL
- `client` text NULL
- `project` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.contact  (152 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `full_name` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `account_ref` text NULL
- `company_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.opportunity  (221 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stage` text NULL
- `amount` double precision NULL
- `customer_number` text NULL
- `account_name` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_missing_relations_v2_src.project  (112 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `status` text NULL
- `go_live` text NULL
- `client_id` text NULL
- `opportunity_ref` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
